text: We present a case of a fifty-two-year-old gentleman who was diagnosed with high-grade dysplasia on colonoscopy in the setting of long-standing ulcerative colitis. After the initial laparoscopic total abdominal colectomy with endoleostomy, he was scheduled for completion proctectomy and J-pouch creation. Intraoperatively, he was found to have a very short mesentery and a J-pouch was not feasible due to a major reach issue and the procedure was converted to an S-pouch creation. In cases where the J-pouch is not feasible due to reach, an S-pouch is a good alternative and can provide an additional two to four centimeters in length.

The surgeon constructs the S-pouch using three limbs of small bowel segments, each measuring twelve to fifteen centimeters. The segments are approximated with continuous 3-0 Vicryl sutures, taking seromuscular bites. The exit conduit, initially four to five centimeters long, is downsized to two centimeters to prevent long-term obstructive defecation. The surgeon begins the aneurotomy on the first limb, staying close to the suture line to prevent serosa puckering and ensure a greater pouch circumference.

The surgeon opens the middle lumen, providing an intraluminal view of the posterior wall. Closure is performed from proximal to distal using two sutures, which are tied in the center to ensure secure closure without relying on a single suture.

The surgeon uses a continuous 3-0 microbe suture on the next limb of the S-pouch, leaving two corner sutures in the proximal distal corners to aid in the creation of the anterior wall. The anterior wall is then sutured with running seromuscular bites, followed by reinforcement with interrupted thoracovacral sutures.

The surgeon downsizes the exit conduit to two centimeters, carefully assessing the blood supply and checking for arterial bleeding, which is crucial for anastomotic healing. An air leak test is conducted, confirming no leaks.
The performed procedures are listed here. The patient was DC'd home after an uneventful recovery, and he underwent an ileostomy takedown after a normal GGE, and is doing well in follow-up.

J-pouch is the preferred form of ileopouch anal anastomosis. Studies have shown its safety and feasibility. The S-pouch is technically more elaborate and needs to be hand-sewn and is also more time-consuming. During IPAA surgery, sometimes creating a tension-free anastomosis with a J-pouch might not be feasible due to reach issues, especially in an obese male with a narrow pelvis. After the standard steps to achieve extra length have been undertaken, if reach is still an issue, an S-pouch might be a good alternative to a J-pouch. Although technically more challenging, the S-pouch gives the patient an opportunity for restoration of intestinal continuity and an ostomy-free life.
